clean_story: Fleeting focus.  Brains filtering fast. "Attention span" measures how long someone can concentrate on a task before their mind wanders—like a flashlight beam that flickers or shifts. If attention_spans can rightly be perceived as a muscle, it’s one that tires quickly in today’s world. In 2000, the average was 12 seconds. By 2023, studies pegged it at 8 seconds—shorter than a goldfish’s 9-second benchmark. Here’s why:Digital screens deliver dopamine hits—likes, swipes, notifications—training brains to crave novelty every few seconds. It’s like snacking on candy instead of a meal. Each ping rewires focus circuits to expect instant rewards. Example:A TikTok video averages 3 seconds to hook viewers. Compare that to reading a book page—2 minutes of sustained focus. Yet attention isn’t just shrinking—it’s fragmenting. Multitasking splits focus into thinner slices. Research shows switching tasks can cost 40% productivity loss per shift. The fix?Deliberate practice. Monks meditating for years expand attention spans to hours. Even 5-minute daily focus drills rebuild endurance. Three related subjects are neuroplasticity, dopamine loops, and deep work.
